record.description: Lois Benvenuti discusses her life growing up in North Adams, Massachusetts, and the difficulties her family went through as the Great Depression hit. She discusses her Italian and Austrian family lines. She recalls hearing about the attack on Pearl Harbor and the job opportunities that came soon after. Benvenuti recalls her brief time in art school before a job opportunity as an aircraft mechanic in Presque Isle, Maine moved her up north. She discusses her love for the job but after the war, her path in that career came to a halt. Lastly, she briefly discusses the end of the war, her marriage and children, and her time in Los Angeles, California.
record.transcript:
DUNHAM: Thank you for having us today. It's July 7, 2014, and this is tape one, with Lois Benvenuti, here in North Adams, Massachusetts. We usually start at the beginning, so what is your full name and date of birth?
BENVENUTI: My full name is Lois Adams Benvenuti, born August 22, 1926. Can you believe?
DUNHAM: Coming up on a birthday.
BENVENUTI: Yeah, I'll be eighty-eight in, what, a month? Less than a month.
DUNHAM: Exciting. Where were you born?
BENVENUTI: Born here in North Adams, and I think in the hospital. I'm not positive about that because a lot of my generation was born at home, but I think I was born in the hospital.
DUNHAM: Did you have siblings?
BENVENUTI: I have two, a brother and a sister.
DUNHAM: Younger or older?
BENVENUTI: Younger. I was born during the Depression, and my mother waited about five years before she had another one. So, my brother is five years younger and then my sister is another two years younger than that.
DUNHAM: Can you tell us a little about your mother and father, and maybe even family background beyond that? When your family immigrated to the United States, if you know?
BENVENUTI: My mother's family--my grandmother and grandfather--were born in--my grandfather's going to hate me--in Italy. He always said to me, tu non sei Italian; sono Austrian, because when he was born, that part of Italy was Austria. His passport was Austrian, so he never considered himself an Italian.
DUNHAM: All right, well, you've put that on the record. [laughter]
BENVENUTI: They were born and brought up in Italy. My oldest aunt was born in Italy, and then my grandmother came over. My grandfather came over before her, naturally, and worked and sent money back home, like they all did. After my aunt was about a year, year and a half old, something like that, my grandmother and the baby immigrated to the United States.
DUNHAM: So your mom was born here?
BENVENUTI: Yes.
DUNHAM: In North Adams?
BENVENUTI: In North Adams.
DUNHAM: What about on your father's side, do you know?
BENVENUTI: My father's side? My father is first generation. He came over, all right? He has a sister that stayed in Italy, and he had two brothers that came over with him, so the three brothers immigrated to the United States.
DUNHAM: How old was your father when he came over?
BENVENUTI: Oh, God, I think he was about fourteen, fifteen, something like that?
DUNHAM: Do you know what it was like for him when he and his brothers got here?
BENVENUTI: I don't know. He didn't say too much about that. He stayed with his older brother because I think his older brother came over first, and then he came over with my grandfather and they stayed with my older uncle.
DUNHAM: Where were they? Were they already in North Adams?
BENVENUTI: Adams.
DUNHAM: Do you know how your mom and dad met?
BENVENUTI: I don't know. I know my mother was working for a dentist, and I'm not sure if that's how they met or if they met through friends. I know there was some connection with the dentist.
DUNHAM: Was there a significant Italian American community here in Adams, North Adams?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah, quite a big one.
DUNHAM: What did your father do? It was the Depression time when you were born and your siblings, so how did they make ends meet?
BENVENUTI: My father owned a restaurant with another guy. I don't know if I should be telling you this.
DUNHAM: Well, as I said, you'll have a chance to review the material, and if you want to seal anything, that's fine.
BENVENUTI: This was during Prohibition, too, okay? My father was a rum-runner. He would run and get the rum and bring it back, and his partner in the restaurant would then sell it, and then Dad would make another trip. Well, the partner in the business was not an honest man either, so eventually, my father just gave it up because he had nothing out of it, and he went to work in a factory.
DUNHAM: Do you know what kind of factory?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, the Jones Division--they printed material.
DUNHAM: Where was it? Was that here in Adams?
BENVENUTI: Adams, yeah. I'm telling you all our secrets.
DUNHAM: Well, this is the real history. As I said, this is both for the current history but also the long term for historians, and that's the reality of how people got by and did things. And certainly during the Prohibition, it's not like there wasn't that reality.
BENVENUTI: Yeah, how we got by.
DUNHAM: So, what do you remember about growing up here, your early years?
BENVENUTI: I remember it was very hard. My mother had to work, because she lost everything and went way into debt when the stocks crashed. So she went to work for the post office, and Dad continued working at Jones Division. Very nice story about my mother: she tried out for the post office for years and kept taking the test and passing it, getting one of the highest grades, and they kept passing her over for gentlemen, the men, because back then, women working--and during the Depression men should have the job and not the women. So, she wrote to Eleanor Roosevelt and explained the situation. You know that the next time they took the test and she passed, she got the job.
DUNHAM: Well, that was probably all the response she needed, but do you know if she got a response directly from Eleanor Roosevelt?
BENVENUTI: Oh, she got a response, but I don't know what she did with it. We searched through all her papers when she passed away.
DUNHAM: Oh, that's too bad, but you know the story, and that's amazing. So, did she work at the post office many years?
BENVENUTI: She worked at the post office until she was about seventy. She didn't want to stop.
DUNHAM: Was that here in Adams or North Adams?
BENVENUTI: North Adams. The first woman ever to be hired at the post office.
DUNHAM: Once she got the job, did she get any grief there?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yes. She was given all the dirty jobs, working nights and loading the trucks. They didn't want her. They didn't want a woman there.
DUNHAM: She shared with you some about how hard that was? Wow, did she ever think about giving it up, or was she determined to not?
BENVENUTI: No, after she got into the crowd and they accepted her, she loved it there because she met the public. She could help the Italians with their business, and she spoke some French so she could help the French. She didn't know any Polish--there are Polish people around--she didn't know any Polish, so she couldn't help them.
DUNHAM: So, Italian, Polish, French, what other ethnicities were in the area?
BENVENUTI: In this area? Well, the Polish people mostly are in Adams, great big Polish population there. In North Adams, there was mostly French, Irish, and Italian.
Fukumoto: So, how was that? Did everybody get along, or were there brawls and fights?
BENVENUTI: You know, as far as I know, we all got along except when my grandmother and grandfather bought the house on Furnace Street, the whole, well, we called it Hickey Hill. "Hickey" is the slang for Irishman, all right? The whole hill, the whole area was Irish settlers, and when they bought the house there, they were the first Italians. Then, one by one, the Italians came in, and the Irish moved out.
DUNHAM: Did the Irish give them any grief, then, when they first--?
BENVENUTI: No, because some of the families that lived there when I was growing up are still living there, their children are.
DUNHAM: Yeah, so not all the Irish moved out, but a lot of them did.
BENVENUTI: A lot of them--most of them.
DUNHAM: Did they move to just another neighborhood, or leave the area altogether?
BENVENUTI: Oh, no, different neighborhoods.
DUNHAM: What languages did you grow up speaking in the home?
BENVENUTI: Italian and English, of course. My mother always said, "This is the United States." We learned English first, and then you can speak Italian, but you learn English first.
DUNHAM: But you did get fluent in Italian as well?
BENVENUTI: With my grandmother, she couldn't speak any English.
DUNHAM: Did you learn to write Italian as well?
BENVENUTI: A little bit, yeah. In fact, we had a reunion over in Italy about ten years ago, and about seventy-two of us got together and went over and visited my uncles and cousins over there, got together with them. So, we all got along. Well, not all of us knew Italian, but we all got along, between gestures and--
DUNHAM: Sure, very nice. What was school like when you were growing up here? What was your schooling, what was an average day at school, grammar school, say? What kind of school was it?
BENVENUTI: It was a very friendly school. There were eight grades? Eight grades, I think. We didn't have any junior high then. It was one to eight and then the high school. It was a very nice school, very small school. I remember the principal used to always catch me chewing gum, and the gum was not always fresh, let me tell you, because we'd take it out at night and put it on the bed, and the next morning, we'd take it and start chewing it again. She--and I have to laugh; my kids enjoy this--had a little poem that she used to make me say every time she caught me chewing gum: "A gum-chewing girl and a cud-chewing cow / There is a difference, I will allow / But what is that difference? I don't know / It's the intelligent look on the face of the cow." [laughter] This is how many years ago, and I still remember.
DUNHAM: Wow, did you write that down, Candice? Candice is a middle school teacher. Oh, that's funny. So, gum chewing, and wow, did you get in any other trouble besides that? Or was that it?
BENVENUTI: Well, I was always lollygagging going to school, late quite a bit of the time. We'd get cracked across the knuckles.
DUNHAM: Oh, wow. Was this a Catholic school? Or just a public school?
BENVENUTI: No, no, no, public school. They don't do that now, but I know we used to go, {inaudible}. See, the principal would grab our hands and say, "You know you were late." That didn't stop us.
DUNHAM: What was your commute to school like?
BENVENUTI: It was about a half a mile, three-quarters of a mile.
DUNHAM: Which you walked?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yes.
DUNHAM: And the winters here get a little--
BENVENUTI: In the winter you walk, yeah. And you go home for lunch and come back.
DUNHAM: Was your mom working? So, were you making your own lunch or how did that work?
BENVENUTI: Well, in the beginning, when I was in until about the third grade, I guess, she used to have a housekeeper who would come in and clean the house for her and make lunch for us kids.
DUNHAM: Since you were the oldest by several years and your mom worked, did you have to have much of a helping out with the little ones?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.
DUNHAM: So that was quite an obligation?
BENVENUTI: And the housework, yeah.
DUNHAM: Can you tell us a little bit about that, what that was like?
BENVENUTI: I thought my sister and my brother were brats, but I didn't dare touch them because they'd go squealing to Mom. So, I'd just tell Mom when she got home how they behaved, and she would take it from there. It wasn't bad; it really wasn't bad.
DUNHAM: I know you later went to art school for a time. Did you have an early interest in art while you were in school?
BENVENUTI: My mother thought I did. I had a choice of going to art school or going to state teachers' college. I mean, I didn't have a choice at all, really. I thought, "Well, I got to get away from North Adams, so I'll take art school."
DUNHAM: What made you want to get away from North Adams?
BENVENUTI: Because there was really nothing to do here, even then. I looked around, and I thought, "I don't want to live here for the rest of my life." I'm back. [laughter]
DUNHAM: Well, let's back up because you were only a first year in high school when the attack on Pearl Harbor happened, right, in the fall of '41. Do you remember first hearing of that?
BENVENUTI: Yes. I remember hearing the President on the radio, and I remember, oh, my God. I thought we were going to get invaded, and really, it really scared you. Then, as the war increased and got bigger and stronger, you could tell that really nothing would happen to the United States, but all our soldiers were going overseas, and I thought that was too bad. That was horrible.
DUNHAM: Also being in a predominantly Italian-American community, were there any tensions or conflicts there? Did you know of any Italian Americans who were incarcerated or taken away at any time?
BENVENUTI: No. We were primarily Americans of Italian descent.
DUNHAM: Right, but you had a lot of first generation in the community, and you had family members back in Italy. So, were there any talk--?
BENVENUTI: There were a few who thought Mussolini was a pretty nice guy. He did a lot for Italy--he put in new roads and new transportation systems, and most of us would look and say, "Yeah, but what about the other things?"
DUNHAM: Had you heard of the Proclamation 2527, signed by President Roosevelt, which did declare Italians enemy aliens, or the Executive Order 9066, which did order evacuation, again, of ultimately mostly Japanese on the West Coast?
BENVENUTI: Oh, the Japanese. What a shame that was.
DUNHAM: But some Italians and German Americans here on the East Coast, yeah, in smaller numbers.
BENVENUTI: Really? I didn't know that.
DUNHAM: So, there was that potential. Let's see. Sorry, just a second.
BENVENUTI: Huh, I never knew that.
DUNHAM: So, within that--and I don't want to belabor this too much--was there any sense within the community of any shame of being Italian during that time?
BENVENUTI: No. I take that back. My grandfather, but not during World War II, during World War I, my grandfather, who was Austrian, right, used to befriend the new Italian families coming in to the area. During World War I, the Italians were really very nasty to the Austrians. They would throw stones at the windows and not really hurt them particularly but make it very uncomfortable for them. It wasn't just my grandfather; it was the other Austrians who lived in the area, too. My grandfather--that's when he said to me, "Tu non sei italiano; sei Austrian." Don't want to have anything to do with those dirty Italians.
DUNHAM: Though your community was ultimately more Italian than Austrian, is that fair to say?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Did you know many Austrians, though?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah.
DUNHAM: Yeah, okay, there were a lot of Austrians, okay.
BENVENUTI: In fact, once a summer, we'd go to--I can't remember the name of the town, but we have all the Austrians from Massachusetts and I think Vermont, Connecticut, from this area, we get together and have a picnic. Still do. Tyrolians.
DUNHAM: Were there many Germans in the area?
BENVENUTI: I don't think so.
DUNHAM: Did you ever hear of any German Americans having any problems during the war or being incarcerated?
BENVENUTI: No.
DUNHAM: Because especially if they were a smaller group, then maybe they might have been--just like the Italians and Austrians got into it--but especially being Germans during the war, don't know. So, your grandmother and grandfather lived here as well. What was life like for them when you were growing up?
BENVENUTI: My grandfather had a job, worked. They had to learn English. In fact, I have a book that showed them how to speak English. I can show it to you.
DUNHAM: Sure, I'd love to see it when we're done, yeah.
BENVENUTI: But my grandmother stayed home most of the time. She couldn't speak English, so what they would do, the neighborhood, some of the ladies would get together and have a little kaffeeklatsch, but it was all in Italian. If she needed something at the store, because I was one of the oldest kids, I would have to go to the store for her.
DUNHAM: Did you have to play translator role much for them or anyone else?
BENVENUTI: Not too much, because most of the people who visited her talked Italian.
DUNHAM: What about health care or a doctor?
BENVENUTI: No. My aunts usually did that. If she needed something really serious, then they would come down.
DUNHAM: When you were growing up, then, when you weren't in school or taking care of your siblings, which may have been a lot of the time, what did you do for fun around here?
BENVENUTI: For fun? We used to bike ride. We'd go to Adams, and then we'd go to Williamstown. We'd walk up to Mount Greylock. That's pretty hefty. I couldn't do it now, but we used to do it, this friend and myself used to do it two or three times a summer. We'd have a little bag lunch and start out in the morning and get up there about four o'clock in the afternoon and rest. Then we'd turn around and come back down. We didn't have to worry then about anybody hurting us. Even if we got home when it was dark--
DUNHAM: Yeah, because you must have got back pretty late if it was that big a hike, yeah. It's very beautiful up there, yeah.
BENVENUTI: Oh, it's beautiful. Beautiful. We used to love to do that. Think of it now.
Fukumoto: Did you have a good relationship with your mom? What was your relationship like with your mom? Were you close? Was she loving, affectionate, or was it--?
BENVENUTI: Not very affectionate. She was like my grandfather in many ways, a very stern German. I always thought of my grandfather as German, very stern.
DUNHAM: The Austrian, it's similar, yeah.
BENVENUTI: Well, he's up there. On the top, the second one in. You could pick him out right away, huh? [laughter]
Fukumoto: Yes, he looks tough. I mean, she sounds like she was very strong, strong-willed.
BENVENUTI: I thought of him more of a German than Italian.
DUNHAM: How were they, the Italians in your family, different?
BENVENUTI: Italians were all huggy-huggy-hug. He was very standoffish. If you swept the floor, you had to do a good job. If you did anything, you had to do a good job.
DUNHAM: So, was your mom's whole side of the family kind of like that?
BENVENUTI: My mother was, my grandfather, and I'd say two aunts were like that. My grandmother was huggy-huggy and one aunt was sort of huggy-huggy.
DUNHAM: How about your dad's side, then, say?
BENVENUTI: Oh, my dad's side of the family was all huggy-huggy.
Fukumoto: What did you prefer? Who are you more like, then? What would you say, are you more like your dad's side?
BENVENUTI: My dad.
DUNHAM: So, what year in high school did you start working at Sprague?
BENVENUTI: My junior year.
DUNHAM: Yeah, how did that come about?
BENVENUTI: I don't know why they ever hired us. I think I was fourteen going on fifteen?
DUNHAM: So, how did you find out about the job, or how did that come to be?
BENVENUTI: Oh, because everybody in school was saying, "You can get a part-time job and earn money," so we went up, and they hired us. Looking back on it, a fourteen and fifteen-year-old kid working? Going to school and working part-time? It was only maybe three hours a day or something.
DUNHAM: What kind of work were you doing?
BENVENUTI: Regular; nothing that took brains, I'll tell you. It was all mechanical.
DUNHAM: Did it come easily to you? How did you learn to do it? Did you have training?
BENVENUTI: No, just went in, and they showed you how to do it, and you did it.
DUNHAM: What were you working on? What type of equipment was it?
BENVENUTI: Capacitors and resistors, and it was stuff that they used, I guess, in the war.
DUNHAM: What was your role in that? What kind of equipment were you using?
BENVENUTI: Mostly a machine like a stamping machine.
DUNHAM: Was it like an assembly line kind of thing?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: So it was pretty monotonous?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: And was it girls and boys working there? Men and women?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. Younger kids, though. There were very few men around.
DUNHAM: Were there ever any injuries on the job? No?
BENVENUTI: No, never heard of any.
DUNHAM: How did you have time to do your homework?
BENVENUTI: Well, I was pretty good in school, and I had all the requirements in to graduate from high school at the beginning of the senior year. So, I took whatever courses I wanted. What I did do was to talk them into letting me go to the machine shop and work in the machine shop.
DUNHAM: So what was that like?
BENVENUTI: Learning the trade. I can't remember now.
DUNHAM: This is at Sprague, or at school?
BENVENUTI: No, school. They were training the kids to work in a factory.
DUNHAM: Were many other girls working in the machine shop?
BENVENUTI: One other. One other one. In fact, she graduated with me, and the last time we had a reunion, she came up to me and said, "I'm so glad you're still alive. So am I. Remember when?"
DUNHAM: Did she end up doing any defense industry work, or would you know what kind of work she did?
BENVENUTI: I don't know because after high school I left.
DUNHAM: So, the war's in full swing at this point. How is life changing in this area, in North Adams, during that time?
BENVENUTI: Oh, you couldn't get anything. You had stamps to get food, you had stamps to get shoes, you had stamps to get almost everything. If you didn't have the stamps, you couldn't buy.
DUNHAM: So, what things were you missing? What things were in short supply that you were used to having?
BENVENUTI: Meat, mostly. Meat and butter. Everything else, we always had a great big garden, so we always had loads of food. Between my mother and I, we'd can, so we had it all winter long, too.
DUNHAM: What did you grow in the garden?
BENVENUTI: Oh, God, what didn't we grow? Corn and beans and peas and lettuce.
DUNHAM: Was that hard work, too, that you participated in?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, well, it wasn't so much hard work, it was hot work. Being out in the sun because I don't like the sun at all. I like it to shine, but I like to sit in the shade, yeah.
DUNHAM: Can you tell us about the canning, then?
BENVENUTI: Oh, God, they used to take all summer long, all our free time. It really cut into the time that we kids had for play. It's amazing, because I talk to my brother and sister, and they say, "No, it didn't bother us." They were just that much younger, where it didn't bother them.
DUNHAM: Like they didn't have to do it?
BENVENUTI: No, they didn't have to do it.
DUNHAM: Was that just during the war years that you did the canning, or had you done it before?
BENVENUTI: No, my mother did it because of the--
DUNHAM: Depression, yeah.
BENVENUTI: Because we were short on cash all the time.
DUNHAM: You mentioned too that as far as rationing, that sometimes there were things you could get up in Canada.
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah. When I was working at Presque Isle. We used to get in the car--we'd save our gas because you had the stamps for gas, too--and we'd all go up into Canada and come back pregnant. [laughter] The girls would come back pregnant; we'd have the meat and the butter under.
DUNHAM: Would they check you when you came across the border?
BENVENUTI: No, no.
DUNHAM: Wow, well, we'll talk more about your time there a little bit later, but I just wanted to ask. Since cash was so short, too, you said that most of the merchants, because they were short of cash, wouldn't extend credit, right, during that time. So you just--again, back to having your own gardens and getting by. Do you remember any particularly tough times or times where it was scary or you didn't have enough to eat?
BENVENUTI: If we didn't have the money, we just didn't buy. Everybody was in the same position, so it wasn't like you were made to feel poor or deprived or anything. Everybody was in the same position. So, it was just normal.
DUNHAM: Did you have big family meals?
BENVENUTI: We always ate together.
DUNHAM: With your grandmother or grandfather?
BENVENUTI: We would do that maybe once a week, yeah.
DUNHAM: Not a particular ritual of where that was necessarily, though, but you would get together routinely, but the immediate family always ate together?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. The big holidays, of course, everybody would get together, but other than that, if we were around and Nona was cooking, she'd say, "Sit down and eat." You pull out a dish--I still do it here, if I'm cooking and somebody comes in, I'll say, "Have you eaten? Do you want something to eat?"
DUNHAM: Did you learn to cook, growing up?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, but not very much because my mother was a terrible cook. If she couldn't fry a piece of meat and open a can, we had sandwiches. My father was a cook. Oh, boy, we used to look forward to weekends because he would do all the cooking.
DUNHAM: What types of things did he cook?
BENVENUTI: Oh, a lot of chicken because we had chickens. Some beef, but not very much beef. Mostly chickens, deer, game.
DUNHAM: Would you catch the deer local yourself?
BENVENUTI: He would go hunting, yeah, he would go hunting.
DUNHAM: How else was the war affecting the community in terms of fellow students or older friends enlisting or being drafted?
BENVENUTI: All of the boys we knew, especially maybe seniors or ones who had just graduated. They were disappearing very fast.
DUNHAM: I see for a time you had a housekeeping/babysitting job? Or is that after you left for art school?
BENVENUTI: Oh, no, that was my mother's--that was if I wanted to go to art school, she couldn't afford for me to go, so she found this lady in Boston who needed a babysitter/housekeeper. So, I did that for room and board.
DUNHAM: How was that, then?
BENVENUTI: It wasn't bad, except that the kid never wanted to go to sleep at night. I didn't really get that much time in for studying.
DUNHAM: How old was the kid?
BENVENUTI: He was about three or four, just enough to be pesky.
DUNHAM: So, you started to tell us before--so at Sprague, were you able to save money, or were you mostly having to give it to your family?
BENVENUTI: Give it to the family. Save money?
DUNHAM: Did you get any allowance, kind of spending money?
BENVENUTI: No. Well, yeah, if we really wanted something, my father would pass us a quarter or a dime.
DUNHAM: What type of thing might that be for? What type of thing might you get the quarter or dime for?
BENVENUTI: Oh, like a Pepsi or maybe an ice cream cone. Nothing big. I didn't think children were allowed in the stores to buy things, because who had money? The kids never had any money; it was just the grownups. For a long time, I thought only grownups could go in the store and buy.
DUNHAM: Did you follow news of the war through newspapers, radio, movie newsreels? Was there a movie theater in this area?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah. At that time, we had three. We don't have any, now, but we had three then. The newsreels were always full of the war and the fighting.
DUNHAM: Were you able to go to the movies often, then?
BENVENUTI: Probably a couple times a month, yeah.
DUNHAM: Was there live music or dances or that type of thing in the area?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. We used to have Glenn Miller and some of the big bands here. My mother used to make me go, and I didn't know how to dance. I didn't particularly want to go. I must have been about thirteen, fourteen, then.
DUNHAM: Did you try to dance? Did you enjoy it when you went?
BENVENUTI: No, I didn't. I used to take a deck of cards with me, and a couple of us girls would sit on the side and play cards.
DUNHAM: So you weren't a big fan, at least at that time, of those things?
BENVENUTI: No, no.
DUNHAM: Before we move on to when you finish high school, is there anything else about those years you'd like to share before you first got out of North Adams, if you will?
BENVENUTI: I don't think so. You know just about everything.
DUNHAM: I guess you were still here--do you remember when Italy surrendered, and if there was any sort of impact or feeling within the community in '43?
BENVENUTI: I don't know--when did Italy surrender?
DUNHAM: I think it was '43, or mid-'43, I think.
BENVENUTI: No. I remember when they hung Il Duce and his sweetie up by the feet.
DUNHAM: What do you remember about that?
BENVENUTI: I remember because my father said, "Oh, my God, they went right through my town." They did, they went up Lago di Garda, up that area and went straight through the area he was from.
DUNHAM: How did he feel about it?
BENVENUTI: He was not too fond of Il Duce. He really wasn't, because he thought he got in with the wrong crowd.
DUNHAM: So, when did you graduate high school?
BENVENUTI: Nineteen-forty-three.
DUNHAM: The summer of '43, okay. It was that point that you had the choice of state teacher college or art school, is that right? Were those two choices that you were interested in?
BENVENUTI: No.
DUNHAM: What would you have wanted to do at that point, if you could have done anything?
BENVENUTI: I was more interested in designing houses. Architect. I really liked engineering, that aspect, but the girls didn't do that kind of thing. Girls don't take up engineering. "What's the matter with you?" I wanted to go Embry-Riddle School in Florida, and my mother said, "Girls don't become fliers."
DUNHAM: Did you talk with your father about it, too?
BENVENUTI: My father was a very easygoing man. Whatever my mother wanted.
Fukumoto: Did you argue, or were you just, that's what she said, that was it?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, I never heard them argue.
DUNHAM: Did you argue with your mom, though?
BENVENUTI: Not very often, because if we talked back to our mother, we got slapped across the mouth. You don't talk like that to your mother.
Fukumoto: Have you ever, though? Have you ever had a slap across your face? Do you remember what happened?
BENVENUTI: I just remember being very upset because they wouldn't listen to what I wanted to say. But it was always my mother's way. I don't have very many fond memories of my mother. I do of my father.
DUNHAM: Your siblings were a lot younger. Did you have friends who you could sort of talk to about those kind of frustrations? Did they have similar realities?
BENVENUTI: They had some too, yeah.
DUNHAM: Well, so, it was art school it was.
BENVENUTI: Mass School of Art, down in Boston.
DUNHAM: What was that like?
BENVENUTI: It was enjoyable. I enjoyed it. I still dabble in it every now and then, and I've taken art courses on my own, at the colleges.
DUNHAM: What kind of art?
BENVENUTI: What do I enjoy mostly? I enjoy sculpture, mostly. I've always been very handy with my hands, and I enjoy--just like gardening, getting out there and getting in the dirt. So, I enjoyed that, mostly. Then, I liked, I guess, pastels better than anything.
DUNHAM: What was it like being out of North Adams, finally? Had you had a chance earlier to travel some out of North Adams on any trips?
BENVENUTI: Trips? No, dear.
DUNHAM: I'm just checking. I know times were tight, but no, not at all.
BENVENUTI: Well, I take it back. My mother and father had some friends down in Hartford, Connecticut, and we went down once or twice, I can remember, to see them.
DUNHAM: Had that been exciting, going somewhere else? Or Hartford was not so--?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: But you had a notion there was more outside of North Adams. Where did your idea of what was bigger than North Adams come from? Was that in the movies and the newsreels, or in books and art?
BENVENUTI: Books, mostly.
DUNHAM: What kind of books made impressions upon you?
BENVENUTI: Oh, God, I still read--I get the National Geographic, and even now, I go through those, and I think, "I've never been there; would it be nice to go?"
DUNHAM: Yeah, we do the same thing.
BENVENUTI: I've sort of stopped traveling.
DUNHAM: So, Boston, did it meet your expectations? What was it like?
BENVENUTI: It was like another country. It was frightening at first, really, because I didn't know anybody there. I've never been to a big city before. I take it back. I was in New York once when my cousin lived here, but it was like, "Wow, I don't know how to get around this place. What do I do? Who do I ask?" But eventually, you learn.
DUNHAM: Did you get there right when school started?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: So, that's when you had the housekeeping/babysitting job at first, so was that pretty close to school?
BENVENUTI: No, that was a good hour and twenty minutes.
DUNHAM: So, what was that commute? How did you do that commute? Was that walking or bus?
BENVENUTI: Bus, yeah.
DUNHAM: That's quite a ways.
BENVENUTI: Yeah. Well, that was my mother's way of saying, if you want to go to school, you've got to work for it.
DUNHAM: Was it also her way of maybe protecting you, having you live with a family, maybe?
BENVENUTI: No, because she didn't even know this family. I don't know how she found this woman's name, but I remember she didn't even go down with me. It would have been nice if I'd had somebody to introduce me, but the woman met me where? Train station in Boston? I can't even remember, now. Mrs. McCutcheon. I'll remember that.
DUNHAM: Was she nice to you?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. Her husband was in the service, and she had a job playing the bass fiddle at night somewhere, and she needed somebody there to watch her child and also to do the housework.
DUNHAM: So, you were specifically working nights with the child. So did you have weekends free?
BENVENUTI: No. It was seven days and weekend.
DUNHAM: Weekend days, did you have free?
BENVENUTI: No, always at night.
DUNHAM: Okay, but what about in the day, during the weekend? Did you have a chance to go explore then?
BENVENUTI: No. That's when I tried to get caught up on all the homework.
DUNHAM: So, how was it? You'd done well in school to then. Was this challenging?
BENVENUTI: It was challenging, but it got so I got behind. I don't know, I just didn't want to make the effort to get caught up because I was very interested in aviation.
DUNHAM: How long were you in art school?
BENVENUTI: A year and a half.
DUNHAM: Maybe before we talk about that transition, you did leave--you moved to the YWCA in Boston, and so, what was that transition like?
BENVENUTI: Oh, that was an eye-opener.
DUNHAM: How so?
BENVENUTI: How so? Well, it was freedom. Oh, my gosh, I could do whatever I wanted, whenever I wanted. It was like saying goodbye to restraints.
DUNHAM: Right, so what did you do with your newfound freedom?
BENVENUTI: Went with the girls to different places. Explored Boston. We used to go down to the North Shore and have pizza, and we'd go to different places.
DUNHAM: You had a line about the girls who had a room facing the street. Did you have a room facing the street?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yes. My room had a flagpole, okay? Across the street, there was a Coast Guard Academy.
DUNHAM: So, if they won't come out, we can take a shot.
BENVENUTI: We used to look out my window and watch the Coast Guard coming in and going out. Everybody knew the window with the flagpole. You can see the flagpole.
DUNHAM: Okay, there it is. So, your window is the second or third floor?
BENVENUTI: My window is the one we're looking out of, and this is the flagpole that was there.
DUNHAM: Oh, I see, this is looking out towards, okay. So, you called them the Coasties?
BENVENUTI: Coasties. Coasties across the street, yeah.
DUNHAM: So, did you ever date Coasties?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah. Well, yeah. In fact, this is a picture of one, Ross Litell.
DUNHAM: Okay, so where would you go out with the Coasties?
BENVENUTI: We'd go down to Revere Beach; we'd walk around Boston. I mean, they didn't have any money either. We'd sit in the Y. I don't know what you call it, not a family room, but like a family.
DUNHAM: Community room or something.
BENVENUTI: We'd all sit there and talk and gab.
DUNHAM: This was a lot of new freedom, but were there some rules kind of restricting fraternization?
BENVENUTI: Not fraternization, but at the Y you had to be in by a certain time.
DUNHAM: You had a curfew, okay.
BENVENUTI: And no boys up to the room.
DUNHAM: Did that rule ever get broken?
BENVENUTI: Not that I know of.
DUNHAM: Or were there ways around that, other places people went sometimes?
BENVENUTI: Well, I don't know what the girls--where they went sometimes. They'd go out goodness knows where. No, we were pretty straight.
DUNHAM: What's the ethnic makeup of both the women's group at the Y and the Coasties?
BENVENUTI: Oh, the women's group at the Y was everything--everything. We had a lot of women living there part-time whose husband was in the service, and when they'd come in, they'd meet in Boston, and then they would leave, and another group would come in. They had everything.
DUNHAM: And from all over the country?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: And the Coasties, too, same thing?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. The guys that I met were from all over. In fact, the one that I have a picture of here that I was kind of sweet on was from California.
DUNHAM: So, what happened with him?
BENVENUTI: I got involved in the Presque Isle, and he didn't want to get serious because he wanted to go back to school, and I said, "Fine, after the war, we'll get together." Only I found somebody else. I don't know about him.
DUNHAM: Had you been dating in high school, back here at North Adams?
BENVENUTI: I really wasn't interested. I graduated at sixteen, so I was a kid, and back then we didn't know anything about sex. We really didn't. We knew there was a difference between boys and girls, but we weren't sure what you did with these things.
DUNHAM: Did you ever get any kind of real instruction about that?
BENVENUTI: No, just don't let boys touch you, and I didn't know where or how or when.
DUNHAM: Yeah, the specifics might be helpful sometimes.
BENVENUTI: In high school, I really thought you could get pregnant by kissing a boy. Man, I didn't get close to them.
DUNHAM: Did you have any fellow students who did get pregnant in high school?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, there were a couple of girls who got pregnant.
DUNHAM: How was that handled, do you know? Were they able to stay in school?
BENVENUTI: No, they didn't stay in school. They didn't stay in school. Back then, that was a pretty serious thing.
DUNHAM: But it happened.
BENVENUTI: It happened, but very seldom.
DUNHAM: Do you know if they stayed in the community?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, they did. The parents took them in. They never left their parents' house.
DUNHAM: Do you know if there were, just thinking within the different ethnic groups, Italian-American girls who may have, versus other groups?
BENVENUTI: I don't know. I don't know because we really never thought about, you're Italian and you're Greek and you're French. It was just, the kids were just kids. We all played together, got angry together.
DUNHAM: Well, aside from meeting the Coasties and having this newfound freedom, how else were your maybe horizons expanded, if you will, by being in Boston, from North Adams to Boston? Were you learning more about the world outside?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. We used to go visit a lot of different places. Maybe we'd go to all the museums around. We were always in and out of the museums, shows. My cousin was in the Navy, and every time he came into Boston we'd go out with him to the nightclubs and things, until they found out I was way underage.
DUNHAM: How did they find out?
BENVENUTI: Because one of them asked me, and Art kept going like this, and I said, "No, what are you saying?" So, I said, "Yeah, I'm seventeen." "You're not supposed to be in here." I wasn't drinking, I was having a Coca-Cola.
DUNHAM: Was the drinking age eighteen or twenty-one?
BENVENUTI: Eighteen. But you know, that's a laugh because our family was brought up with wine and whisky in the house all the time. My father always said, "It's there if you want it, but don't ever let me see you drunk."
DUNHAM: Did you grow up drinking in moderation, then?
BENVENUTI: I never liked the taste of it.
DUNHAM: Me neither.
BENVENUTI: I never liked the taste of it, so yeah, if we were sick my mother would make us a hot toddy with honey and lemon and a shot.
DUNHAM: You could get that down all right? Did it seem to work?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. It took a while.
DUNHAM: Yeah, I think I'm allergic.
BENVENUTI: I thought it was terrible.
DUNHAM: So, you're at the YWCA, and in your second year of art school. The summer, were you in school or were you off, that first summer?
BENVENUTI: I was off. I got a job working for Gillette Razor Company. We had to take the blades and take them out of this container where they kept getting and put them in another place where the package came, and wrap paper around them. I always had cuts all over my fingers from the blades.
DUNHAM: Yikes. So, that was not fun?
BENVENUTI: No, that was not fun.
DUNHAM: Do you remember what the pay was like there?
BENVENUTI: All I know is it just barely paid for the Y.
DUNHAM: Back at Sprague, you said you made about fifty-six cents an hour, part-time, and so this maybe was comparable to that, or something?
BENVENUTI: It probably was a little more, more like sixty cents an hour.
DUNHAM: Were you doing it full-time all summer, then?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, I did it full-time, but only for about two months, I think, a month and a half, two months. Then, I remember one time I had to go to the bathroom, and you weren't allowed. You weren't allowed. They told you when you could leave your station to go to the bathroom. I thought, "Uh-uh. If I have to, I've got to." So, I went up to the lady and said, "I got to go. There's no getting around it." She said, "If you go, you're fired." I said, "Goodbye."
DUNHAM: Wow. So, you had a female boss, but that was it.
BENVENUTI: They did that to everybody.
DUNHAM: How long a shift would it be? Did you have a lunch?
BENVENUTI: You had four hours.
DUNHAM: You had a lunch break, but still, you had to make it four hours with no break, no bathroom, no exception.
BENVENUTI: No breaks, no nothing, yeah.
DUNHAM: Is this like an assembly line that these are coming on?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah.
DUNHAM: Did you do different jobs on the line, or was it always that same--?
BENVENUTI: No, all those razor blades.
DUNHAM: So, was this near the end of the summer when you left under duress?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: So then you went back to school, to begin, in the fall?
BENVENUTI: Went back to school.
DUNHAM: How did it go from there?
BENVENUTI: Well, at that time, Rosie came to the Y and started talking to us girls about, "Oh, it's nice in Presque Isle, and it's a good job, and it's something that you can continue with after." We all thought, "Yeah, that sounds pretty good." So I'd say there were about five of us who went from the Y.
DUNHAM: Now, let me ask you, when you say Rosie came and spoke, can you describe, what does that mean?
BENVENUTI: She was the secretary.
DUNHAM: Oh, and her name was Rosie.
BENVENUTI: Rose, yeah.
DUNHAM: So it's just a coincidence, because we think of Rosie the Riveter.
BENVENUTI: Oh, no. She was from Presque Isle; she was from Caribou really, which is a neighboring town. What she didn't tell us was, it got to be so cold up there you couldn't--we had to walk from where we were staying to the base. There was no transportation. You had to have muffs on. It used to get to be twenty and thirty below zero.
DUNHAM: What had it been like growing up here? What was the coldest it tended to get?
BENVENUTI: I can remember one girl freezing her ears walking. She had a scarf around her head, but she didn't have enough padding.
DUNHAM: That was here, or that was up in Maine?
BENVENUTI: Up there.
DUNHAM: Much colder than growing up here.
BENVENUTI: Oh, God. Yes!
DUNHAM: We're just about out of this tape, so let's take a break, and then I definitely want to hear a lot more about your time up there.
Audio File 2
DUNHAM: This is tape two with Lois Benvenuti, and it's July 7, 2014, and you were just telling us about the presentation that Rose, or Rosie, was making, I guess, at the YWCA. So, remind us about that, then.
BENVENUTI: At the Y, yeah. She came in the beginning of my second year, and she was very enthusiastic about what a lovely job it was, how much you could help the war, because they needed people up there to help with the planes. So, she talked, I think, five or six of us into quitting our jobs.
DUNHAM: Were any other of the YWCA folks in art school with you, or in school elsewhere?
BENVENUTI: No, nobody in art school. They all stayed in art school. They were smart, I guess; they finished their education. The five or six of us signed up, and they sent us to the Academy of Aeronautics in LaGuardia Field. We were there for five months. I don't know how they thought they were going to train us to be mechanics in five months.
DUNHAM: What do you remember about that training?
BENVENUTI: I remember that it was very concentrated, that we didn't have much time to do anything except work on engines and read how to do it.
DUNHAM: The five or six of you came down, and was it a much bigger group that was training?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah, there was a whole group there from all over New York and Massachusetts and the whole area.
DUNHAM: All women, or a mix of men and women?
BENVENUTI: Men and women--boys and women. I don't think many of them were much older than I was, maybe sixteen, seventeen.
DUNHAM: So, was that challenging?
BENVENUTI: It was very challenging, very interesting. I loved it. I loved it. In fact, when I was up there I had decided that I was going to continue with it afterwards and maybe, maybe go down to the Embry-Riddle in Florida and further my education. Well, I found out that there wasn't an airport around that wanted to hire a woman after the war because all the men were coming back.
DUNHAM: You wanted to continue as a mechanic?
BENVENUTI: Probably as an engineer. But I even studied and took the exam for mechanic license, and then when I found out that nobody wanted to hire a girl, I thought, "Oh! I just wasted about a year and a half, two years of my life."
DUNHAM: How did you find that out?
BENVENUTI: Because I wrote to a couple airports and said, "These are my qualifications, I'm interested in a full-time job." They wrote back and said, "Uh-uh, honey, there are too many soldiers coming back, too many men coming back who have had a lot more experience." Which was true.
DUNHAM: So they didn't straight-out say it was because you're a woman; they said more experienced, but same difference, I guess. So, this training at LaGuardia, where are you staying while you're doing that training?
BENVENUTI: Oh, they had a house there that you had to stay in, I think in Johanna's thing, she told you. They had a house that you had to room in, and you walked to the class from there.
DUNHAM: Is this Army?
BENVENUTI: No, it wasn't Army; it was a branch of the Army, I guess.
DUNHAM: ATC, Air Transport Command, yeah. So that's who was running it, but it was civilian?
BENVENUTI: It was all civilian, yeah, except the head honchos. They were military.
DUNHAM: So, how were the instructors with you? Were the instructors all men, or were there some women instructors?
BENVENUTI: None. They were all men.
DUNHAM: I recall your mom's challenge, being the first woman at the post office here. Did you or any of the other women or girls experience any of that kind of hazing, if you will?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. The guys that were in class with us thought we were kind of strange. "You're a girl; what do you want to be a mechanic for?" Because it's a job and because I'm interested.
DUNHAM: Did they give you a hard time beyond that kind of verbal questioning?
BENVENUTI: No. In the beginning, it was like very standoff, "what kind of girls are you,." But after they got to know us we got very friendly. Unfortunately, we knew them for only about five months, and then we were sent all over the East Coast.
DUNHAM: So there had been some dating and getting to know each other and then being spread out?
BENVENUTI: No.
DUNHAM: Oh, not that, but just friends? Were the instructors--the male instructors, did they give you a hard time at all?
BENVENUTI: They thought we were kidding. They didn't think that we were very serious. We had to prove to them that this is what we wanted to do, this is what the Army said that we could do.
DUNHAM: Had there already been other women through this training program?
BENVENUTI: Not too many.
DUNHAM: So, it's a five-month--you're down there; did any of the women or boys quit during that time?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, one did that I know of. She used to be my roommate in Boston. She decided, no, this isn't for her.
DUNHAM: How long did she do it for?
BENVENUTI: She did it for maybe two or three months, then decided no. She was very feminine. I'd been a tomboy all my life, so to me, it was a lot of fun.
DUNHAM: Would you say most of the other women who were doing it were tomboys, or varied?
BENVENUTI: Not really tomboys, but they weren't femininy, femininy, not the nail-painted. Down to earth, yeah.
DUNHAM: Well, you said it was an overwhelming amount of stuff to learn in that short a time, but you also wanted to prove yourselves. How did you accomplish it?
BENVENUTI: We did a lot of studying. You know what the thing was that we enjoyed doing when we had free time? We'd go down to LaGuardia and sit. They had a roof-like, you could watch the planes come in and out. That's what we did.
DUNHAM: That was your break?
BENVENUTI: That was our break. That was our big thing to do.
DUNHAM: So, you weren't doing a lot of clubs, going to clubs, and that type of thing? This was intensive?
BENVENUTI: I never thought that was very much fun, going to clubs and drinking.
DUNHAM: Did you have written exams, physical exams?
BENVENUTI: Physical exams, yeah.
DUNHAM: Can you describe one of those, what it would be like?
BENVENUTI: Well, it was very embarrassing, I remember that, because I've never really had a doctor examine me before then, right? But actually, it was nothing, looking back on it. The written exams, yeah, we used to have pop quizzes and everything all the time.
DUNHAM: It's interesting, you say about the doctor's exam and not having that, when I said physical exam, I guess I meant like a hands-on test for you to do. So what would one of those tests have been like, I guess? Or what were you doing?
BENVENUTI: It was taking engines apart and putting them back together. This is how you do it, and this is why you do it, and if this goes wrong, this is what you're supposed to do. So, it was always a physical thing.
DUNHAM: What models of planes were you working on?
BENVENUTI: Oh, God, you're asking me now? How many years ago was this?
DUNHAM: I didn't know if you might recall. Let's see, I'm looking at Johanna's right now. This is later.
BENVENUTI: C-47s?
DUNHAM: C-54s and C-47s?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: What tools were you using to do all this work? What all tools?
BENVENUTI: We had a toolkit; when we first went up to Presque Isle, we each had a toolkit. We were responsible for the tools.
DUNHAM: So, what all is in an airplane mechanic's toolkit?
BENVENUTI: Everything. Wrenches and screwdrivers and everything. Even hammers, because sometimes you had to bang on something to get it loose. All kinds of tools.
DUNHAM: Do you work in teams or individually?
BENVENUTI: We worked in teams, yeah. There'd be three or four of us working on an engine, pulling it apart or putting it back together.
DUNHAM: Would your teams be all women or a combination?
BENVENUTI: Combination.
DUNHAM: So, anything else about the training at LaGuardia or the time there before we talk about your moving up to Maine?
BENVENUTI: No. It was enjoyable. I really enjoyed it.
DUNHAM: Was there anything particularly challenging? I know it was hard, but was there anything where you got to a point of ever thinking maybe you couldn't?
BENVENUTI: Oh, no, I wanted to eat it up--I loved it. I loved it.
DUNHAM: This was the kind of opportunity you'd really been waiting for.
BENVENUTI: I just loved working with my hands. I always have, so it was really great for me.
DUNHAM: I didn't ask you about this transition and your mom and family. So, did you let them know? How did that go?
BENVENUTI: That I had taken the job? Oh, yeah.
DUNHAM: Well, whatever the sequence of events is, I'm curious, given the history.
BENVENUTI: I called her up one time, and I said, "I just took a job, I'm going to go to LaGuardia Field to train, and then I'm going up to Presque Isle to be a mechanic." She said, "What!" [laughter] She said, "You're going to be sorry you quit school," and I said, "Well, this is something I want to do." At seventeen, what did I know?
DUNHAM: Did she give you her blessing, so to speak?
BENVENUTI: Oh, no. Nope. "You're going to be sorry."
DUNHAM: But she didn't disown you, if you will, or that type of thing? She just said, "You'll be sorry."
BENVENUTI: Oh, no. That was going against her will, so there was no "I hope you make out well."
DUNHAM: Was that hard?
BENVENUTI: No, not really. I was more concerned about what my father would think.
DUNHAM: Did you speak with him, also?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. He said, "Just do a good job."
DUNHAM: So, that was nice to hear.
BENVENUTI: That was like a pat on the back. "Go ahead."
DUNHAM: Had you had opportunity to, or had you been visiting, or had they visited you at all during this first year-and-a-half?
BENVENUTI: At school? Oh, I went home.
DUNHAM: Regularly?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, I went home for vacations. I know my mother said to me, "Well, when does school end because you have to come home," and I said, "I got a job."
DUNHAM: Oh, in summer, yeah.
BENVENUTI: I got a job.
DUNHAM: So, that was partly to retain that independence, then, yeah. Well, so, how was it determined who went where from LaGuardia?
BENVENUTI: They just told us.
DUNHAM: You didn't have a choice?
BENVENUTI: Well, I had a choice between Manchester and Presque Isle. They were going to send me to Manchester, and I said, "Great." I didn't care where they sent me, but there was one guy there who said, "I can't go to Presque Isle, I got to be close to my wife, and she doesn't want to go up to Presque Isle." So I said, "Well, change me, I don't care."
DUNHAM: Little did you know how cold it was.
BENVENUTI: Boy, if I had known was Presque Isle was like, I would have taken Manchester.
DUNHAM: So, what time of year did you go up to Presque Isle?
BENVENUTI: It was fall.
DUNHAM: Not into winter yet.
BENVENUTI: No, it was spring, it was springtime.
DUNHAM: What was it like when you first got there?
BENVENUTI: Very small town, a very small town. I thought North Adams was small? There was a hotel, a restaurant, a post office, a bank, and about six shops. That was it.
DUNHAM: Wow. How many people were working at Presque Isle?
BENVENUTI: Well, there weren't too many civilians. There were enough. I'd say maybe fifty to seventy-five, but it was a lot of military there, yeah, because it was a base for the planes.
DUNHAM: So how many out of the fifty or so were women, do you think?
BENVENUTI: I'd say about almost half of us.
DUNHAM: Most of you arriving all at the same time, starting then?
BENVENUTI: No.
DUNHAM: There were a fair number that were there already?
BENVENUTI: They kept coming in.
DUNHAM: So, do you remember your first day on the job?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, I remember the first day on the job. I remember the first couple of weeks on the job, because my grandfather had become very ill, and he died. Of course, when an Italian grandfather dies everybody goes to the funeral, the wake. They didn't want to let me go. They said, "If you go, then don't come back." I said, "Hey, this is family, I have to go." They said, "Sorry. You go, and you don't come back," so I said, "Okay." So, I had a boss there who was very nice. He said to me, "Come back. I'll see that you have a job." So I did. I went to the funeral, which I was going to do come hell or high water, and then when I went back I had a job.
DUNHAM: When you saw your family at the time, were they asking about--were you updating them about--?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, they wanted to know how things were going. I said, "Great, I love it," And my mother-- [laughs and makes a face] I think she really hoped that I'd flunk, that I'd fail. But after a while, she came up to visit me. She and my father came up to visit me in Presque Isle.
DUNHAM: What were those first days on the job like?
BENVENUTI: Very confusing because I didn't know what I was supposed to do. So, we had a good crew, we really had a good crew. John Evans was one of the bosses, and he just explained everything. He said, "This is what you have to do. Follow me, do what I do, and I'll show you." So, for the first month, whatever John did, we did. Then, after that, we got so we knew that when this plane came in and we had to pull it apart, we knew how to do it.
DUNHAM: Was it fairly routine, that every plane's coming in for maintenance, that you pull it apart? Or would it come in with a specific problem?
BENVENUTI: All different things, yeah, because a lot of the planes were coming in from overseas. They'd hit Newfoundland, and then they'd land; ours was the first base that they would land on that we'd do repair work.
DUNHAM: So, were you working day shift? Was it only day shift, or were there multiple shifts?
BENVENUTI: Multiple shifts.
DUNHAM: What shift were you on?
BENVENUTI: Days, mostly, but then sometimes we would work until one, two o'clock in the morning, that swing shift. Never all night.
DUNHAM: Never graveyard, okay. So, which did you prefer and why?
BENVENUTI: Didn't matter. Really didn't matter, because I didn't like getting up in the morning--I still don't--but I never minded staying up late at night. I didn't care which shift they put me in.
DUNHAM: I know it took a while to get the hand of it, but are there any particular jobs that are challenging, or aspects of working on these planes?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, I had to learn how to--I'm trying to think of the forklift; I used to have to learn how to do the forklift, all the difference machines because we needed them to raise the engine and lower the engines. So, I had to learn all that, and it was like, "I don't know if I can do this."
DUNHAM: Would someone show you first how to do the forklift, but then eventually you're in it by yourself?
BENVENUTI: They'd have to.
DUNHAM: This is a forklift that can--what does it do, exactly?
BENVENUTI: The engine is the size of this table, maybe bigger, so you couldn't possibly, even ten men couldn't possibly lift it. You had to get a forklift.
DUNHAM: What type of accidents or injuries might have happened then, and what kind of first aid or health care did you have around?
BENVENUTI: I can remember a couple people having jewelry and getting the jewelry caught. But I don't remember much else besides that. It was just stupid things, really.
DUNHAM: But you're working with this big equipment, so I just didn't know if something ever fell.
BENVENUTI: We used to have to keep our hair back. No bobby pins. Every once in a while, they would make us go up on a test flight. "You do a good job, you're going to go up on the test flight. Let's see how comfortable you are, going up on a plane that you helped fix." [laughter]
DUNHAM: Were you ever nervous about that?
BENVENUTI: No. I loved it.
DUNHAM: Tell us about that. What was that like?
BENVENUTI: It was a thrill, to go up in a plane that you did most of the work on the engines, and it's running. [laughs] And it hasn't stopped.
DUNHAM: How many people are in these planes? It's the pilot?
BENVENUTI: Pilot, copilot, there were a couple of others. I'm not sure what they did in the plane. And then there were a couple of us.
DUNHAM: Wow, how exciting.
BENVENUTI: Really great. What a great feeling: I fixed this plane and it's actually flying.
DUNHAM: Would the pilots do any kind of tricks?
BENVENUTI: No, you can't do.
DUNHAM: But it was thrilling.
BENVENUTI: I would have loved it. [laughter]
DUNHAM: Did you have an ambition or interest in learning to fly yourself?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah. I really wanted to learn how to fly. Boy.
DUNHAM: Was there ever any opportunity to do that? Had you heard about female pilots at all during the war?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, and actually, a couple times when we went up on the flight to check, the pilot would say, "You want to get in the copilot's seat?" "Oh, do I want to, yes!" But it wasn't something that you would talk about. If I learned how to fly a plane that big, I don't think I could have gotten a license to fly a smaller plane unless I did a lot of studying.
DUNHAM: Were you able to fly in the copilot's seat? Yeah. What was that like?
BENVENUTI: Oh, it was a thrill. It was a thrill.
DUNHAM: I wanted to talk about the rest of life in this small town, but you're just beaming, talking about these experiences. I'm wondering what else you can share about this work in this time period there. What was the social life like when you weren't working? It's a lot of military there.
BENVENUTI: Yeah, we used to go over to the PX a lot, because they had a room over there where you could play records.
DUNHAM: A PX, what's that?
BENVENUTI: It was the place where the soldiers, the whole base could go, you could buy things there or you could buy a snack, kind of snack-like things, or they had a room where you could sit and talk, and they had a room where they had all kinds of music and you could listen to music. Didn't have any movies, but there was a movie in town if you wanted to go. But very seldom did we ever go. Mostly, we just sat and met in the room over at the PX and talked about the day's adventures. Then, one of us had a car, as I said before, and we used to save all the gas stamps because we'd all sign up for them. We'd go over into Canada; we'd take rides. It was really nice.
DUNHAM: That's when, in Canada, they didn't have rationing, so you were able to buy meat?
BENVENUTI: They didn't have rationing. I couldn't understand it. Canada--what, an hour away--didn't have rationing, and we couldn't get anything.
DUNHAM: So what all would you get when you'd go up to Canada?
BENVENUTI: We'd get meat, we'd get butter, because now, by this time, we had some friends in town, and we'd say, "What do you need? We're going over." We'd come back, as I said before, pregnant because we weren't supposed to bring that stuff into the United States.
DUNHAM: But nobody ever really checked or enforced that?
BENVENUTI: Nobody ever checked or stopped us. I mean, we stopped at the border and they'd say, "Have you got anything?" We'd say, "Nope."
DUNHAM: Were you ever nervous about that?
BENVENUTI: No. What were they going to do? They'd take it away.
DUNHAM: So you got there in spring, summer, so things are going well, but then winter is a real bear.
BENVENUTI: Winter was a blister.
DUNHAM: How did you cope with that?
BENVENUTI: Just walk very fast, I'll tell you, walk very fast. Did a lot of running.
DUNHAM: Any slipping?
BENVENUTI: It was sort of slippery, but they kept the sidewalks pretty shoveled.
DUNHAM: You're working indoors?
BENVENUTI: Yeah. Oh, yeah, we worked the hangars. They were indoors, heated. Not warm, warm, warm, but they were heated. We didn't need gloves, mittens.
DUNHAM: Did you have to buy new stuff to cope, though, as far as clothing for when you were outside?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM: Was that hard to get, or was that pretty easy in that area?
BENVENUTI: It was easy to get, but you still had to have the coupons. So, you got pretty cautious about what you bought and how you used it.
DUNHAM: Where were you living now, sleeping?
BENVENUTI: They had taken over--the base had taken over, I think it was a college dorm up in Presque Isle, and that's where the girls stayed. I don't know where the boys stayed, but I know the girls had to stay up there. Had to.
DUNHAM: Were there exceptions to that or ways around that?
BENVENUTI: As far as I know, no. All of us girls were up there.
DUNHAM: I didn't know--the way you said "had to" like that, I didn't know. It was just that it was sort of strictly enforced and told is what you mean?
BENVENUTI: I'm trying to think of when June met this guy and got married, but we really, God, I don't know how to explain it. We were not sexually oriented at that time. We really weren't. We knew boys were there, but it was always like a friendly--it was never sexual.
DUNHAM: But would you say romantic? There's a lot more boys than girls there, right? You would think.
BENVENUTI: No. Oh, we got a lot of offers, got a lot of offers.
DUNHAM: Were there dances? Did you like dancing any more by this time or not really?
BENVENUTI: I got so I liked to dance, yeah. But I'm trying to think of--on the base, they would have dances about once a month. We'd go to the dances.
DUNHAM: Was that with live bands or records or both?
BENVENUTI: I think it was records.
DUNHAM: You talked about having to have your hair back. What uniform did you wear?
BENVENUTI: You know the kerchiefs the guys wear around their necks sometimes? We'd tie that around our heads. We didn't look super-duper.
DUNHAM: Right, the bandana-like. And what was the rest of your outfit like, your mechanic's outfit? I think the white one, yeah, we'll take a photo of it. So, it's a big white--there it is. Okay, yeah, we'll take a shot of that later. You can leave it for now, yeah.
BENVENUTI: These were our uniforms.
DUNHAM: To work, you wore those uniforms. Was women's fashion changing at all yet by this time for what you wore outside of work? You've mentioned you were a tomboy, for example.
BENVENUTI: Slacks.
DUNHAM: You did wear slacks?
BENVENUTI: Right. We had a couple dresses with us in case we wanted to go out somewhere and dress up, but slacks were much more comfortable.
DUNHAM: So all the women were wearing slacks at this time?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Was that true at art school?
BENVENUTI: No. At art school, a lot of them were wearing sweaters and skirts.
DUNHAM: How about yourself? Do you recall?
BENVENUTI: Half and half. If I knew I was having classes, like an art history or something, then I'd get dressed up. But most of the time, if you're painting with paints or you're working with clay, you don't want a dress on.
DUNHAM: What about, going back, growing up in North Adams as a child and young girl?
BENVENUTI: I used to love slacks because you could bend over, you could do anything you want, and never had to worry.
DUNHAM: Did most of the girls around here wear slacks during that time?
BENVENUTI: No. Growing up, no, there were just several of us. Usually, the ones who liked the outdoors, the ones who went hiking, the ones who went bicycle riding. They're the ones who more or less wore the slacks. We'd get dressed up, of course, we had dresses, but for horsing around and for doing things, we wore slacks. I can remember the men saying, "You women don't look good in slacks, you know." Too bad. [laughter]
DUNHAM: That's great. Anything else about your time up in Maine? How long were you there, all told?
BENVENUTI: Oh, gosh. The war was over with, and they were sending, little by little, the whole base home.
DUNHAM: So, you went up in spring of '44? You'd been in art school a year, maybe that took you into '44, and then what time of year was it that you had the five months' training, so that probably went through the winter in LaGuardia, so it was early spring '45 that you went up. So, near the end of the war, actually. So, what do you remember about V-E Day, V-J Day? Do you remember those?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah, everybody went crazy in the streets up there. Everybody. I don't think anybody went to work that day.
DUNHAM: What did you do? What do you remember about it?
BENVENUTI: I just remember joining everybody on the streets and thinking, oh, my God, thank God it's over with, thank God it's over with. Not having a thought in your head about, well, what are you going to do when it's over with?
DUNHAM: When did you begin to have that realization or concern?
BENVENUTI: Well, when I saw everybody leaving, because they really cleaned out the base within six months.
DUNHAM: Were people being laid off?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, and sent back to wherever they originated from. I stayed on because they put me in the office to work in ops, and so I got so I would okay the flights in and the flights out, and God, I can't remember everything I did now. I know we used to have to balance the weights in the plane so that the plane wasn't top-heavy or side-heavy. We had to know how to distribute the weight in the plane.
DUNHAM: Did you like doing that work?
BENVENUTI: Not really.
DUNHAM: That was kind of a letdown, after?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, and it was office work.
DUNHAM: So, how did that transition happen?
BENVENUTI: Well, they said to me, "Do you want to continue working?" I said yeah, thinking I would continue working on the engines. They say, "Well, we've got a job for you in the office." I thought, "Oh, is that a letdown!"
DUNHAM: Was it the same pay, do you recall? Not that that was the most important thing.
BENVENUTI: They sort of sprung it on you little by little. "You like working here?" Yeah. "You like what you're doing?" Yes. "Well, if you want to continue working here, we're going to have to change it around a little bit." And then before you know it, you're working in the office and you think, "This isn't what I want."
DUNHAM: Were there other women and men that you knew working there that wanted to keep their jobs that couldn't?
BENVENUTI: Not too many. There were a few who stayed on, and there were a couple girls who married guys from up in that area because, as the men started coming home, the soldiers and sailors that were from that area came home. The guy I married was a soldier at the base.
DUNHAM: So how did you meet?
BENVENUTI: He walked in and saw me, a mechanic, started a conversation. We just got to know each other. He seemed very nice. He was very nice. He still was very nice even when I left him. He had a terrific personality, really, but we just didn't hit it off right.
DUNHAM: But you did have two children together, so they're all okay?
BENVENUTI: The one on the top is my two sons and their two sons. These are my two grandsons. He's not an Arab.
DUNHAM: Where is he in that photo?
BENVENUTI: One of the motorcycle manufacturers sent him over to a rally, I think you call it, in Arabia. They had people from all over the world testing these motorcycles in the desert. They had a rally, and they sent him, and he sent me back that picture, and I thought, "Oh, God, you look like an Arab."
DUNHAM: You reminded me--you mentioned a little while back that your parents came and visited you up in Maine. So what was that like?
BENVENUTI: It was very pleasant. She had, yeah, mellowed by then. I have to laugh because they said to me, "It gets how cold up here?" They went down and bought me a bottle of Southern Comfort.
DUNHAM: Now, you didn't really like alcohol, but did that help?
BENVENUTI: Southern Comfort wasn't bad.
DUNHAM: Not in negative twenty anyway, huh?
BENVENUTI: It wasn't bad. It was kind of sweet, like. Went down easy.
DUNHAM: Now, were you already dating your eventual husband by the time they came up, or was that later?
BENVENUTI: Just starting.
DUNHAM: So they didn't meet at that time, okay. Well, how did your time in Maine come to an end?
BENVENUTI: I finally decided that I didn't want to work in the office anymore. This was about a year after everybody else had left. John was being discharged from the Army, so he said, "Well, why don't we get married? If I go to California, I don't know when I'm going to get back this way." So I said, "All right, all right, what else?"
DUNHAM: At what point did you apply to the school down in Florida? Was that while you were still up in Maine?
BENVENUTI: I didn't apply to the school in Florida. I wanted to.
DUNHAM: Remind me, you wrote some letters for jobs elsewhere, at airports. Was that while you were still in Maine?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, that was while I was still in Maine.
DUNHAM: Were there any other avenues you were pursuing at this time to look for similar kinds of jobs? Did you feel very discouraged?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, I did because I thought, "God, I have all this training, somebody must want a woman mechanic." But no.
DUNHAM: Did you know of any other women who were still working as mechanics?
BENVENUTI: No. No, we all had to change our ways.
DUNHAM: I know it wasn't spoken of a lot at the time, and I know you've already said you weren't that sexually aware, if you will, but I was wondering, did you know, during any of this time, of any lesbian or gay individuals?
BENVENUTI: No, I didn't. It never dawned on us to think of someone in that respect. I don't think it would have mattered. We were all there together, doing a job.
DUNHAM: So, what happened when you left Maine? You went to Southern California?
BENVENUTI: Yes.
DUNHAM: And when did you get married?
BENVENUTI: We got married up in Maine, and then my mother decided that we weren't married because we didn't get married in the church, so we had to come to North Adams and get married in the church, which was about four months later, I guess it was. Then, we went to California.
DUNHAM: What was California like? Not cold.
BENVENUTI: Oh, I loved it. Absolutely loved it.
DUNHAM: Whereabouts were you?
BENVENUTI: LA, but that's before the freeways. Before the freeways it was a lovely place. Quiet. You could walk--that's thunder, honey. [laughter]
DUNHAM: Not much of it in California.
BENVENUTI: Not much thunder in California. Well, I can remember when I was out there, the only time you got rain was January and February. That's the only time you got rain.
DUNHAM: Especially LA. LA's the desert, really.
BENVENUTI: I can think, oh, my God, what do they do? They have to water their lawns all the time. And then with all the people moving out there, I thought, how are they going to manage that? Well, they dammed up the Colorado, that's how they did that.
DUNHAM: So, what was life like for you in Los Angeles?
BENVENUTI: In Los Angeles? Beautiful neighborhood. It was really an older neighborhood with a lot of old people there, but it was very friendly; everybody was friendly. You could walk the streets. Safe.
DUNHAM: Your husband was going to school on the GI Bill?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: First at Caltech, then USC, is that right?
BENVENUTI: Yes.
DUNHAM: So you had two children? Or you had one child?
BENVENUTI: Had one child out there and one child here in Massachusetts.
DUNHAM: Then things weren't working out, so you returned back here, right? And you've lived all the rest of your life here, back in North Adams?
BENVENUTI: Oh, no, no.
DUNHAM: No, you've been around, okay.
BENVENUTI: I went back to work for Sprague's and saved my money, worked for them for ten, twelve years. I saved my money like crazy, stole from my husband when he was drunk and he didn't know how much money he had, put it all away, and I enrolled in the college here and got a degree as a teacher.
DUNHAM: Yeah, great, and then you worked thirty years?
BENVENUTI: Thirty years, yeah.
DUNHAM: Is there anything else you'd like to share with us today before we close?
BENVENUTI: You know my whole life story. [laughter]
DUNHAM: Well, we focus on those early years, which are remarkable. I mean, I guess I'd like to ask, I know you so loved being a mechanic and wanted to be an engineer or even pilot. Did you have any other efforts through the years to try to pursue something in that realm?
BENVENUTI: No, because I was working full-time to try and bring my kids up and save enough money to send them to college. No extra dough.
DUNHAM: So that was necessary. Thinking back on the war years and the opportunity you did have during that time, how do you think it did impact the rest of your life?
BENVENUTI: I think it made me a lot more independent, and I knew from that that I could take care of myself. I didn't have to depend on Momma and Daddy. So, in a way, that was great. I've done a lot of things since then. We ran the restaurant out in Los Angeles, a little bar connected to a restaurant, or a restaurant connected to a bar. At that time, you had to have food if you had a bar, and the guy who had the bar didn't want to bother with the food. I can remember taking my son, and getting a cardboard box, and putting him in the cardboard box, and putting a sign on the counter, "Please don't make any noise; my son is sleeping underneath."
DUNHAM: Did people respect the sign?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Wow, in a bar/restaurant?
BENVENUTI: Yeah, well, because I did the day shift. John did the night shift, so I cleaned up and made the breakfast in the morning, and lunch. Then about five o'clock I'd take the baby and go home, and John would come and take over.
DUNHAM: During the war years--I hadn't asked before but did you see Rosie the Riveter kind of imagery and that type of thing?
BENVENUTI: Oh, yeah.
DUNHAM: What was your feeling at the time around sort of seeing that and the work you're doing?
BENVENUTI: Well, I used to look at that and think, "How come they don't have any pictures of the gals who used to work on the airplanes?" But then I'd think, "Well, there weren't that many of us, really."
DUNHAM: Well, now we do--we have yours and we have others. There were a number of others. Now, when you were out in LA, did you know about Douglas Aircraft and women who had worked there?
BENVENUTI: No, I didn't know that.
DUNHAM: There were definitely a number of women who worked out there, and I do believe there's one who was able, I think, is still working part-time there today, in a mechanic role. She got a little press, yeah. So, I'll see if I can share--
BENVENUTI: Really? Because boy, she must be in her eighties, too. I'm eighty-eight.
DUNHAM: She is. I think she started young like you, but anyway, I'll see if I can share that article with you about her. But yeah, we definitely have done a number of interviews with mechanics such as yourself, and it's wonderful. And others who, I think, shared that, but did have the frustration of end of the war, end of the line for that opportunity.
BENVENUTI: Yeah, you had to switch gears, you really did.
DUNHAM: Well, is there anything else? You've been out to the national park in Richmond, the Rosie the Riveter. So what was your impression of the park?
BENVENUTI: I thought it was really set up nice, I really did. There wasn't too much there. They were in the process of making the permanent--
DUNHAM: Exhibits, which just opened this last May. You were there about two, three years ago?
BENVENUTI: Yeah.
DUNHAM: We'd love you to come out again if you're up to it and see the exhibits. And soon we'll have your oral history. Any last thought's you'd like to share with us today before we close?
